narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 65% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes being in the bottom decile for Year 1 grades at school, the third decile for Year 3, an LSAT score of 29, an undergraduate GPA of 2.3, coming from an upper-middle-income family, identifying as female and Asian, and planning full-time studies—compared to averages where students typically rank around the middle for school deciles, score 36.7 on the LSAT, and have a 3.21 GPA. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 school ranking, which had the biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure since it was lower than what succeeds for most students. Next in importance was your LSAT score, also well below the typical level that correlates with passing the bar. Your Year 1 ranking came third, similarly contributing to the risk because it placed you in the lowest group compared to peers. Factors like your family income bracket, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender followed in descending order of influence, each nudging the prediction slightly toward failure relative to common successful profiles—though none were as pivotal as your school rankings and LSAT. Overall, your academic standings in school and standardized test performance stood out as weaker than the dataset's typical successful students, who often excel in these areas. While demographics like being Asian or female didn't dominate, they played smaller roles in the context of broader trends. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim higher toward the average of 37, strengthen any upcoming academic performance to boost your school deciles, and consider programs or prep that address GPA gaps if possible through post-baccalaureate options. Building a stronger academic track record will directly counter these top risk factors. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these areas—keep pushing, and reapply with those improvements. We're here to support your journey.
